turns.content: I've been using fat LTO in CMake before, so I thought setting lto = true in Cargo.toml would give me the same aggressive cross-crate inlining that flattens everything into a single monolithic object file.

That's actually a common mistake because Rust's cargo uses thin LTO by default even when you set lto = true. Thin LTO performs optimizations within crates but doesn't fully merge them like fat LTO does, so cross-crate inlining remains limited compared to what CMake produces with fat LTO flags.
But I tested it and my binaries are still growing larger than expected. Is there a way to force Rust to behave like CMake's fat LTO without manually managing LLVM bitcode files?

You can achieve more aggressive optimization by using lto = "thin" in Cargo.toml combined with the linker flag -C link-lto, but even then Rust defaults to thin LTO which trades binary size for compile speed. To get true fat LTO behavior without external tools requires enabling full cross-crate inlining through specific compiler flags that go beyond standard cargo configuration.

I've been working with the Rust toolchain for a while, and I'm thinking LTO is just like how we used to optimize in C by passing flags directly to the linker. It feels like setting lto=true in Cargo.toml should be enough to get that aggressive cross-crate inlining without any extra steps.

That's a reasonable analogy, but there's a key distinction here: Rust's LTO isn't just a simple flag toggle for the final link step. When you set lto=true in Cargo.toml, it triggers full-link-time optimization which does perform cross-crate inlining and removes dead code across all crates in your workspace, but it comes with significant trade-offs. The real difference is that Rust's implementation uses the rustc compiler to generate optimized intermediate representations before linking, whereas traditional C linkers just apply post-hoc optimizations on separate object files.

So if I just set lto=true, it should automatically inline everything across my workspace? I thought that was what made LTO so powerful compared to thin LTO options. But I'm worried about compile times because I haven't tested the actual performance impact yet.

You're right to be cautious about compile times. Setting lto=true does enable full-link-time optimization which includes cross-crate inlining, but it dramatically increases compilation time and memory usage during the build phase. The Rust compiler needs to materialize all intermediate representations before linking, which is why many projects use lto="thin" instead. Thin LTO enables some optimizations like inter-procedural analysis but skips the expensive full-link-time step, giving you a better balance between optimization gains and build performance.

Okay, so thin LTO is basically a middle ground that trades off some cross-crate inlining for faster builds? But I want to make sure I understand exactly what lto=true does versus the thin option before I commit to changing my Cargo.toml configuration. Is there a specific scenario where full LTO becomes necessary despite the compile time penalty?

Exactly, you're hitting on the right trade-off point. Full LTO (lto=true) is typically reserved for scenarios where binary size optimization is critical and build times are acceptable overhead, like in system libraries or performance-critical tools. For most application crates, lto="thin" provides sufficient benefits without the steep cost of full-link-time optimization. The Rust toolchain handles this distinction automatically based on your Cargo.toml setting, so you just need to weigh whether your project's build pipeline can afford the extra time.
